claims.claim: The first video game is an inadequate question because electronic play, computer play, raster-TV apparatus and commercial industry have different thresholds.
claims.contradiction_notes: Does not dismiss OXO, Tennis for Two or Spacewar! as non-games.

claims.claim: The pioneer-patent argument concerns a television-type raster apparatus with player-controlled symbols, hitting, coincidence and response.
claims.contradiction_notes: Earlier games remain historically important but differ technically and legally.

claims.claim: Sinclair was central to UK home computing, but the BBC/Acorn educational route and Commodore route also shaped the industry.
The ZX Spectrum display used 6,912 bytes: 6,144 bitmap bytes and 768 colour-attribute bytes, creating colour-attribute constraints.
Printed listings made program text a distribution channel, lesson, installation, debugging exercise and modifiable source.
claims.contradiction_notes: Do not imply print was the only route; include cassette swapping, shops and clubs.

claims.claim: Tynesoft is associated with Blaydon and a move from educational software into games/publishing.
claims.contradiction_notes: Official company records still required for exact legal names and dates.

claims.claim: Tynesoft makes businessification visible through mastering, duplication, packaging, artwork, reviews, retail, support, ports, licences, wages and risk.
claims.contradiction_notes: Omit unverified personal allegations and use recollection as testimony.
claims.public_location: Tynesoft case study and business pipeline

claims.claim: The Tynesoft staff photographs now have source-attributed provenance through Kevin Blake's Stairway to Hell article, but definitive public face labels remain under verification.
claims.contradiction_notes: Source captions are testimony, not a fully corroborated public left-to-right identification.

claims.claim: A retrospective account recalls a Washington Galleries computer club and later Oxclose Community Centre as meeting places for early programmers.
claims.contradiction_notes: Needs documentary corroboration before stronger public language.
claims.public_location: Before the Studios and Research Status

claims.claim: Icon, Audiogenic/ASL and several North East authors are connected in surviving recollections, but the nature of any organisational transfer is unresolved.
claims.contradiction_notes: Do not display as legal succession.
claims.public_location: Icon/Audiogenic case study and lineage view

claims.claim: Early Reflections should be presented through BBC/Electron work and Superior relationships before later Driver/Ubisoft history.
claims.contradiction_notes: Early details need stronger contemporary sources.

claims.claim: Tynesoft-to-Zeppelin is currently a staff/founder heritage lead, not a proven legal succession line.
claims.contradiction_notes: Companies House supports the later Zeppelin/Merit/Eutechnyx name chain, not every origin claim.

claims.claim: Teesside should be researched as a separate regional orbit rather than collapsed into Newcastle/Tyneside.
claims.contradiction_notes: Optimus/Iguana/Acclaim details need source replacement beyond the poster.
claims.public_location: Teesside case study and regional map

claims.claim: Modern North East examples such as Atomhawk, Ubisoft Reflections and People Can Fly Gateshead should be epilogue evidence, not proof of one uninterrupted lineage.
claims.contradiction_notes: Avoid a corporate directory overwhelming Phase 0.

claims.claim: Newcastle's Game Technology Lab bridges regional skills, game-engineering teaching, industry relationships and the later university story.
claims.contradiction_notes: Phase 1 will handle the lab in detail.
